metadata.title: The Infrastructure of Good: What 17 HAI Projects Reveal About Change

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Much unfairness is information asymmetry dressed up as a meritocracy. Fixing it requires education that closes the gap, not advocacy that only names it. Only 44.6% of these graduates are employed versus 62.1% of domestic graduates. Projects like RAMAN Effect wastewater surveillance build durable infrastructure rather than awareness.

artifactLines: Much unfairness is information asymmetry dressed up as a meritocracy.
Fixing it requires education that closes the gap, not advocacy that only names it.
Only 44.6% of these graduates are employed versus 62.1% of domestic graduates.
Projects like RAMAN Effect wastewater surveillance build durable infrastructure rather than awareness.

beats.narration_text: The framing that stuck with me is information asymmetry dressed up as meritocracy, fixed by education rather than advocacy. I want to see how that plays out across the projects. Show me: (1) how a single number like 44.6% employed versus 62.1% for domestic graduates exposes the asymmetry, (2) how a project like the RAMAN Effect wastewater surveillance actually builds infrastructure instead of just raising awareness, and (3) what made Wilkes shipping a finished article from raw footage within days an example of the same pattern.

beats.narration_text: The RAMAN Effect project is advancing AI-enhanced wastewater surveillance for pathogen detection at the population level. Wilkes published a finished article for an Indian nonprofit—Homes of Hope India—within days of receiving raw footage.
